figures.caption: Figure 1: Online surrogate training workflow with OGAS. All components are running in parallel.
Table 1: Diffusion generator and density-ratio classifier configuration.

Figure 5: Gray-Scott trajectory grid. Visual snapshots from the 2D validation suite: Gray-Scott (reaction-diffusion patterns). All simulations are performed using the Exponax JAX-based spectral solver [23] and show timestep evolution across different domain size values

Figure 6: Navier-Stokes trajectory grid. Visual snapshots from the 2D validation suite: Navier-Stokes Kolmogorov Flow (fluid dynamics). All simulations are performed using the Exponax JAX-based spectral solver [23] and show timestep evolution across different diffusivity values

Figure 7: Kuramoto-Sivashinsky trajectory grid. Visual snapshots from the 2D validation suite: Kuramoto-Sivashinsky (chaotic). All simulations are performed using the Exponax JAX-based spectral solver [23] and show timestep evolution across different domain size values
Figure 8: Example of truncated fourier IC sampling with various cutoff and taper power
Figure 9: Example of gaussian blobs IC sampling with varying number of blobs
Table 3: 1D experimental configuration.
Table 4: Buffer size and resampling cadence ablation on 1D KS. Smaller buffers with faster refresh yield better robustness (lower Max and Std). Best values in bold.
Table 5: Tail quantile and mixture rate ablation on 1D KS

Table 6: Summary across PDEs and sampling strategies (reported as mean ± half-range). OGAS = main method with ratio debiasing, OGAS-NC = no ratio debiasing, OGAS-R = no ratio debiasing but trained only on uniformly sampled lambdas. Best (lowest) per PDE and metric is in bold.
Table 7: Improvement ratio vs Uniform across Architectures. Values are Mean ± Std. 1 is no improvement, higher than 1 is improvement, lower than 1 is degradation
Table 8: Improvement ratio vs Uniform across PDEs. Values are Mean ± Std. 1 is no improvement, higher than 1 is improvement, lower than 1 is degradation
Figure 10: Navier-Stokes Trajectory Samples. Comparison of rollout predictions on difficult trajectories. We compare the ground truth (GT) against surrogates trained with Uniform sampling and OGAS

Figure 11: Kuramoto-Sivashinsky Trajectory Samples. Comparison of rollout predictions on difficult trajectories. We compare the ground truth (GT) against surrogates trained with Uniform sampling and OGAS.
Figure 12: Gray-Scott Trajectory Samples. Comparison of rollout predictions on difficult trajectories. We compare the ground truth (GT) against surrogates trained with Uniform sampling and OGAS.
Figure 13: Mixture sampling evolution for Gray-Scott.
Figure 14: Mixture sampling evolution for Kuramoto-Sivashinsky.
Table 9: 2D Navier-Stokes Results. Active learning strategy comparison on Kolmogorov flow. Values show mean ± std across seeds. Best per architecture in bold.
Figure 15: Mixture sampling evolution for Navier-Stokes.
Table 10: 2D Kuramoto-Sivashinsky Results. Active learning strategy comparison. Values show mean ± std across seeds. Best per architecture in bold.

Figure 16: DDPM Training Loss Evolution (OGAS-L). The plots show the training loss (log scale) over optimization steps for the OGAS-L strategy, averaged over 3 seeds with standard deviation indicated by the shaded regions. Comparison is performed across three architectures: FNO, U-Net, and scOT.
Table 11: 2D Gray-Scott Results. Active learning strategy comparison. Values show mean ± std across seeds. Best per architecture in bold.

Table 12: Narrower-range sensitivity check (UNet, N =104, 2D, 3 seeds). OGAS-L vs. Uniform on KS and GS with reduced domain-size ranges. Lower values are better; “Rel.” is the relative reduction of OGAS-L over Uniform.

Table 13: Single-Model Navier-Stokes Results. Evaluation is performed one single model trained or on the first model of the ensemble when ensemble is required for uncertainty computation. Values show mean ± std across seeds. Best per architecture in bold.

Table 14: Single-Model Kuramoto-Sivashinsky Results. Evaluation is performed one single model trained or on the first model of the ensemble when ensemble is required for uncertainty computation. Values show mean ± std across seeds. Best per architecture in bold.

Table 15: Single-Model Gray-Scott Results. Evaluation is performed one single model trained or on the first model of the ensemble when ensemble is required for uncertainty computation. Values show mean ± std across seeds. Best per architecture in bold.
